Speaker 1: And the next thing I want quickly talk about is the different between relationship and
transactions. A lot of people nowadays they email for transactions. They email for sales
and it's very evident when you get an email and you know the headline says free, free,
free! Money machine, money machine as Dean talks about, you know everyone wants
to guarantee the overnight success. That's not what emails need to be in today's day in
age, because everyone's used to it. 'Cause it's not just marketers or entrepreneurs or
experts nowadays, you know you have the Cook Polls of the world, the Expedias, the
Amazons who are just blowing people up nonstop with those very buy, buy, buy emails.
And what you want yours to be is you want to email them as if you're talking to a best
friend. What I've noticed over the last three to four months that I've been, uh, obsessing
on for Dean is changing all of our emails to be as relationship driven as possible.
Speaker 1: Dean and I write all the emails, uh, hand in hand, but when we write them, we write
them as if we're talking to our best friend. We'll write them as if we're, we're sending it
to our COO, someone that we're close with in the office or maybe a sister, brother,
parent. And you would be shocked at how much more people want to communicate
with you and they went to learn from you, listen to you, buy from you when they feel
like they're just chatting with them. It it's really powerful, so always remember in all
your email marketing, you have to remember the hook, story, close, but you also have
to remember that those hooks, stories and close have to be relationship driven and not
transaction driven. If your end goal is the money, then you'll never succeed in this, in
this business. You have to have the intention of changing lives and having a relationship
with your students.
Speaker 1: A lot of people always ask what time of day to send, what day of the week to send? I
don't really think there's a science to it as much as everyone wants to say there is, but
what I will say is that whatever you decide to send once you have an email list or once
your building an email list, is to find consistency. Uh, for years we sent an email every
Monday morning, uh, to Dean's list, we called it, uh, the weekly wisdom. And it was
powerful and everyone wanted it. If we missed a Monday, people would literally ask,
where is my email? So it's really the consistency that's important and it also doesn't
matter necessarily when you send the email an exact time. Some systems like, Maropost
will let you actually dictate- sends when the person is most likely to open it, but for
everything else. I like to send in the morning because it gives a lot of the international
people a chance to open to because of the time zone. But I don't- honestly don't think
you're gonna be too hurt if you open a little bit later.
Speaker 1: So, that's one of the things I get asked all the time, is day and time. I think there- there's
very little corelation between the two as long as your not sending and email at like
midnight Pacific standard time. That would be one recommendation I would definitely
have.

world for three years and the biggest thing I took away from it- Doesn't matter where
